stem: The linear transformation \( T : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2 \) has standard matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \), so \( T(\mathbf{x}) = A\mathbf{x} \). If \( \mathbf{v} = ([[v1]],\ [[v2]]) \), what is the FIRST coordinate of \( T(\mathbf{v}) \)?
solution: \( T(\mathbf{v}) = A\mathbf{v} \); its first coordinate is \( [[a]]\cdot[[v1]] + [[b]]\cdot[[v2]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the SECOND coordinate of \( T(\mathbf{v}) \) (using row 2 of \(A\)), not the first.
This swaps \( v_1 \) and \( v_2 \) when applying row 1 of \(A\).
This subtracts instead of adds the second term.
This adds the coordinates of \( \mathbf{v} \) directly, ignoring the matrix \(A\) entirely.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.

stem: The linear transformation \( T : \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}^2 \) is given by \( T(\mathbf{x}) = A\mathbf{x} \) with \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \end{bmatrix} \). If \( \mathbf{v} = ([[v1]],\ [[v2]],\ [[v3]]) \), what is the SECOND coordinate of \( T(\mathbf{v}) \)?
solution: \( T(\mathbf{v}) = A\mathbf{v} \); its second coordinate uses row 2 of \(A\): \( [[d]]\cdot[[v1]] + [[e]]\cdot[[v2]] + [[f]]\cdot[[v3]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the FIRST coordinate of \( T(\mathbf{v}) \) (using row 1 of \(A\)), not the second.
This reverses which entry of \( \mathbf{v} \) multiplies which entry of row 2.
This subtracts instead of adds the last term.
This adds the coordinates of \( \mathbf{v} \) directly, ignoring the matrix \(A\) entirely.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.

stem: The linear transformation \( T : \mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R} \) is defined by \[ T(x,y,z) = [[a]]x + [[b]]y - [[c]]z + [[d]]x - [[e]]z. \] What is \( T([[x0]],\ [[y0]],\ [[z0]]) \)?
solution: Combining like terms, \( T(x,y,z) = ([[a]]+[[d]])x + [[b]]y - ([[c]]+[[e]])z \). Substituting \( (x,y,z)=([[x0]],[[y0]],[[z0]]) \) gives \( T = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of the \(z\) terms instead of keeping them negative.
This adds the input coordinates directly, ignoring the coefficients of \(T\).
This only keeps the \(x\)-terms of \(T\), dropping the \(y\) and \(z\) contributions.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( T \) be a linear transformation with \( T(\mathbf{u}) = ([[p1]],\ [[p2]]) \) and \( T(\mathbf{w}) = ([[q1]],\ [[q2]]) \). If \( \mathbf{v} = [[m]]\mathbf{u} + [[n]]\mathbf{w} \), what is the FIRST coordinate of \( T(\mathbf{v}) \)?
solution: By linearity, \( T(\mathbf{v}) = T([[m]]\mathbf{u}+[[n]]\mathbf{w}) = [[m]]T(\mathbf{u}) + [[n]]T(\mathbf{w}) \). Its first coordinate is \( [[m]]\cdot[[p1]] + [[n]]\cdot[[q1]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the SECOND coordinates of \( T(\mathbf{u}) \) and \( T(\mathbf{w}) \) instead of the first.
This subtracts instead of adds the second term.
This adds the given first coordinates directly, ignoring the coefficients \( m,n \).
This adds the coefficients \(m,n\) directly, ignoring the images of \( \mathbf{u},\mathbf{w} \) entirely.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.

stem: Let \( S : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2 \) have standard matrix \( \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \), and let \( R : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2 \) have standard matrix \( \begin{bmatrix} [[e]] & [[f]] \\ [[g]] & [[h]] \end{bmatrix} \). If \( \mathbf{v} = ([[v1]],\ [[v2]]) \), what is the FIRST coordinate of \( (R \circ S)(\mathbf{v}) \)?
solution: First compute \( S(\mathbf{v}) = ([[s1]],\ [[s2]]) \). Then \( (R\circ S)(\mathbf{v}) = R(S(\mathbf{v})) \) has first coordinate \( [[e]]\cdot[[s1]] + [[f]]\cdot[[s2]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses row 2 of \(R\) instead of row 1, giving the SECOND coordinate of \((R\circ S)(\mathbf{v})\).
This applies only \(S\) to \(\mathbf{v}\) and forgets to apply \(R\) afterward.
This applies only \(R\) directly to \(\mathbf{v}\), skipping \(S\) entirely.
This swaps the two coordinates of \(S(\mathbf{v})\) before applying row 1 of \(R\).
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
